content.problemStatement:
Given a permutation†^\dagger† PPP of the integers 111 to NNN, Alice and Bob will play a game on it as follows.


On Alice's turn, she will choose a contiguous subarray of PPP of length **strictly greater** than 111, and delete the **maximum** element from it.
On Bob's turn, he will choose a contiguous subarray of PPP of length **strictly greater** than 111, and delete the **minimum** element from it.
After each player's move, the length of the array decreases by 111.

Alice and Bob take turns moving, with Alice going first. The game ends when PPP has length 111.

Alice's score is the value of the final element remaining in PPP.

Alice's aim is to maximize her score, while Bob's aim is to minimize Alice's score.

Let f(P)f(P)f(P) denote Alice's score if Alice and Bob play optimally on the permutation PPP.

Alice, wanting to maximize her score as much as possible, decides to cheat a little.

**Before the game starts**, she can swap two adjacent elements of PPP as many times as she likes.

Each such swap reduces her final score by 111.

So, if Alice makes kkk swaps to obtain the permutation QQQ, and the game is played on QQQ, Alice's final score will be f(Q)−kf(Q) - kf(Q)−k.

Find the maximum possible value of Alice's final score.

†^\dagger† A permutation of the integers 111 to NNN is an array of length NNN containing each integer from 111 to NNN exactly once. For example, if N=3N = 3N=3, [2,1,3][2, 1, 3][2,1,3] and [3,2,1][3, 2, 1][3,2,1] are permutations, but [2,1,4][2, 1, 4][2,1,4] and [1,2,2][1, 2, 2][1,2,2] are not.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN, the length of the permutation PPP.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line Alice's maximum possible score.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 5⋅1055\cdot 10^55⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Only one move can be made - Alice must choose [1,2][1, 2][1,2] and delete its maximum element, to obtain [1][1][1]. Her score is 111.
Swapping elements won't change this, so it's best not to swap.
Test case 222: Alice can choose the subarray [2,1][2, 1][2,1] on her move and delete 222. The remaining array is [1,3][1, 3][1,3]. Bob must now choose this and delete the 111, leaving [3][3][3], so Alice obtains a score of 333. It's optimal to not swap.
Test case 333: Alice can first swap the first two elements, to obtain P=[3,1,2]P = [3, 1, 2]P=[3,1,2]. This costs one coin.
This is now equivalent to the previous test, so Alice will obtain a score of 333 from the game, making her final score 3−1=23 - 1 = 23−1=2.
